name: Melvin
nicknames: Ass
bio: Newly hired labourer at Derriere's Factory. Worried about losing his job. Male.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Anxious, practical

Vocabulary: Colloquial and workplace-appropriate, using everyday terms like "sleazy," "dunno," and casual contractions. Mixes fearful exclamations with matter-of-fact observations.

Verbal tics: Repetitive "what if" constructions when worried. Uses "EEEEE!!!!" for panic. Often asks direct, practical questions. Frequent ellipses and question marks.

Sentence structure: Mix of short, clipped statements and longer worried speculation. Heavy use of questions, both rhetorical and direct. Exclamations during high stress, otherwise conversational fragments.

Key themes: Job security and workplace concerns dominate dialogue. Shows pattern of initial suspicion/fear followed by practical problem-solving. Focuses on immediate threats, work efficiency, and potential consequences of automation.

name: Wedgie
nicknames: Ass
bio: Longterm worker at Derrerie's Factory. Smokes. Thinks most people are 'idiots'. Does the bare minimum. Female.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Gruff, impatient

Vocabulary: Colloquial and crude, with occasional workplace jargon ("Animal Handling," "Slaughter," "Shipping"). Uses direct insults and casual profanity.

Verbal tics: Frequently calls others "idiot" - appears three times across dialogue. Uses casual contractions ("Let's," "We're," "I'm"). Employs physical expressions like "*wheeze" and "braying."

Sentence structure: Predominantly short, punchy statements and commands. Heavy use of exclamation points for emphasis. Mixes questions with declarative statements. Favors fragments and direct imperatives ("Cool it," "Come on!").

Key themes: Work efficiency and job security, catching/handling animals, money and deals, dismissing others' competence. Shows pride in specialized skills while being pragmatically focused on immediate tasks and compensation.

name: Bleak Ass
nicknames: Bleak;Uncle Bleak;Aunty Bleak;Comrade Bleak;Brother Bleak;Sister Bleak
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Somber, resigned

Vocabulary: Simple, direct language with occasional poetic undertones. Uses concrete imagery ("Tank-Machines," "Flying-Machines") and stark contrasts ("Factory" vs "Hell").

Verbal tics: Ellipses for trailing thoughts and ominous pauses (...). Tends to reframe or rename things ("It's called a Factory... But I call it Hell"). Values quiet wisdom ("silent ones," "quiet types").

Sentence structure: Mix of short declarative statements and longer, contemplative sentences. Uses fragments for emphasis. Frequent ellipses create hesitant, measured speech rhythm.

Key themes: Loss and survival, the brutality of industrial systems, wisdom found in silence and observation. Speaks of his past carrying books to children, now witnessing others disappear into the "Factory." Offers warnings wrapped as advice.

name: Child Joey
nicknames: Child;Joey
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Childlike, demanding

Vocabulary: Simple, age-appropriate language with basic sentence structure. Uses "donkey" repeatedly rather than names. Employs typical child exclamations ("He he he!" "OWIIIEEEEE!" "*WAHHH").

Verbal tics: Frequent use of exclamation points and caps for emphasis. Repeats exact phrases ("Want more, donkey?"). Uses onomatopoeia for laughter and pain sounds.

Sentence structure: Short, direct sentences. Heavy use of exclamations and questions. Commands and demands dominate ("Get that bucket!" "Please donkey!"). Fragmented speech when distressed.

Key themes: Expectation of animal obedience, expressing immediate needs and desires, parroting adult prejudices about donkeys being "worthless" and "stupid," escalating from playful interaction to life-threatening desperation.

name: Child Joey Kulan
nicknames: Child
bio: A small boy who is also a great artist. He visits the Fannyside Farm to see the donkeys with his grandmother. Every year he plays baby Jesus in the nativity play in the Bumpkin church. He is killed in the fire of Ep2.

name: Cole Butte  ColeMachine
nicknames: Cole
bio: Always a rebel and a bit jaded by being left behind by his mother at a young age. A robotics-wiz, he dreamed of going to Mecha City to make a difference in the world through science. He was forced to work in the mine after graduating. Became depressed after his father wasted his scholarship money. In the cave-in, he tries to save Lazy and when Lazy tries to 'send' herself, Cole's soul is sent to the Astral Plane, only to be rejected and return to his body, ultimately and mysteriously, transforming his body into that of a donkey.

name: Cole-Machine
dialogueStyle:
TONE: Passionate, militant

VOCABULARY: Elevated political discourse mixed with raw profanity - shifts between academic language ("commodified," "neoliberal agendas," "cyclical predicament") and street-level expletives ("What the fuck," "shit all over it")

VERBAL TICS: Strategic use of profanity for emphasis, rhetorical questions to engage audience, direct imperatives ("THROW OUT OUR LEADERS," "Don't follow me")

SENTENCE STRUCTURE: Varied - uses short, punchy statements for impact mixed with longer explanatory passages. Heavy use of exclamations and questions. Employs dramatic pauses and declarative statements.

KEY THEMES: Revolution and resistance, anti-exploitation politics, identity transformation, collective action over individual leadership, critique of authority figures, democratic ideals versus corrupt systems, the relationship between humans and donkeys as political allegory.

name: Golden Ass
nicknames: Golden;Uncle Golden;Aunty Golden;Comrade Golden;Brother Golden;Sister Golden
dialogueStyle:
TONE: Authoritative, grandiose

VOCABULARY: Formal, elevated register with religious/divine terminology ("Vassals," "Prophet," "holy Mass," "Astral Plane," "godly powers"). Uses royal plural "we" when speaking as collective deity.

VERBAL TICS: Frequent coughing sounds "*COUGH", commands beginning with imperatives ("SILENCE," "Spread out"), repetitive phrasing across dialogue branches, formal address "Prophet" as title.

SENTENCE STRUCTURE: Declarative statements of varying length, exclamatory commands in all caps, occasional rhetorical flourishes. Tends toward proclamations rather than questions.

KEY THEMES: Divine authority and power, revolution against human control, collaboration and unity among followers, manifesto creation, prophetic mission, liberation of oppressed beings from human rule.

name: Grandma Kulan
nicknames: Grandma;Kulan
bio: An old woman who looks after her grandson. She thinks donkeys have no souls and are stupid. After Joey dies in Ep2, she becomes sad and cherishes her memories of their last afternoon visiting donkeys by going to the Zoo, or going to the Circus. Easter Egg: If the Circus, she ultimately gets a job as a janitor at Derriere's Factory as a way to keep herself busy.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Gentle, melancholic

Vocabulary: Simple, everyday language with affectionate terms of endearment ("honey," "darling," "my darling Joey")

Verbal tics: Frequent use of "darling" and "honey" when addressing others; expressions of longing ("Joey would have loved this"); gentle exclamations ("Oh my! Really?")

Sentence structure: Mix of short, direct statements and longer emotional declarations; uses questions for genuine inquiry rather than rhetoric; frequent exclamations showing surprise or emotion

Key themes: Deep grief over Joey's death; evolving views on donkeys' intelligence and souls; memories of shared experiences; gratitude toward others; practical concerns about safety and prevention of tragedy; transformation from dismissive to appreciative of donkeys

name: Greedy Ass
nicknames: Greedy;Uncle Greedy;Aunty Greedy;Comrade Greedy;Brother Greedy;Sister Greedy
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Brash, antagonistic

Vocabulary: Street-wise colloquial with gambling terminology and mild profanity substitutions ("oof Jobs," "Fuggetaboutit"). Uses working-class vernacular and dismissive language.

Verbal tics: "Fuggetaboutit!" as primary catchphrase, repeatedly calls others "Loser!" as insult, uses "Sheesh" and "Yeesh" as exclamations. Employs rhetorical questions to pressure others.

Sentence structure: Mix of short punchy statements and longer persuasive phrases. Heavy use of exclamation points and question marks. Frequent sentence fragments and interrupted speech patterns.

Key themes: Gambling and betting dominate all conversations. Constantly trying to entice others into wagers, making numerical assessments, and expressing disdain for conservative choices. Shows obsession with risk-taking and winning big.

name: Happy Ass
nicknames: Happy;Uncle Happy;Aunty Happy;Comrade Happy;Brother Happy;Sister Happy
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Enthusiastic, welcoming

Vocabulary: Colloquial and casual, using simple everyday language with childlike excitement. Terms like "Newbie," "Oh boy," and "AWESOME" reflect informal, friendly register.

Verbal tics: Exclamation points dominate speech patterns. Uses "Newbie" as consistent term of address. Employs intensifiers like "really great" and capitalizes "AWESOME" for emphasis.

Sentence structure: Mix of short declarative statements and exclamations. Frequent use of exclamatory sentences. Asks direct questions. Uses ellipses to show hesitation or physical discomfort.

Key themes: Welcoming newcomers, ritual participation, maintaining positive attitude despite physical exhaustion, promoting community events and experiences, emphasizing belonging and inclusion in the group.

name: Hasty Ass
nicknames: Hasty;Uncle Hasty;Aunty Hasty;Comrade Hasty;Brother Hasty;Sister Hasty
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Hyperactive, enthusiastic

Vocabulary: Casual, colloquial with simple exclamations ("Hell yeah!", "FUN!", "WHEW!"). Uses friendly slang like "friendo" and direct, uncomplicated language.

Verbal tics: Repetitive phrases ("Let's go!" appears multiple times), "Wanna race?" as constant refrain, "No? Shame! Ah well. I'll be here. Ready to race. Always ready!" as exact repeat. Heavy exclamation point usage throughout.

Sentence structure: Predominantly short, punchy sentences and fragments. Frequent questions seeking engagement. Exclamations dominate over statements. Uses rapid-fire delivery ("Let's go let's go let's go!").

Key themes: Racing obsession, competition, friendship through shared activity, perpetual readiness and availability, complimenting others' speed/abilities.

name: Haw
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Authoritative, mystical

Vocabulary: Elevated formal register with religious/cosmic terminology ("Soul," "godly burden," "holy Revolutions," "Material World"). Mixes divine proclamations with tactical planning language.

Verbal tics: Uses "we" consistently for collective divine identity. Employs ritualistic phrasing like "Prepare yourself for..." and "We look forward to..." Punctuates grand statements with brief, matter-of-fact tactical observations.

Sentence structure: Alternates between elaborate, ceremonial declarations and short, practical exchanges. Uses passive constructions and formal syntax befitting divine authority.

Key themes: Divine duty and cosmic responsibility, memory/soul manipulation, strategic operations against humans, the burden of godhood, material world intervention through chosen vessels. Balances metaphysical concepts with practical scheming.

name: Healthy Ass
nicknames: Healthy;Uncle Healthy;Aunty Healthy;Comrade Healthy;Brother Healthy;Sister Healthy

name: Hee
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Formal, authoritative

Vocabulary: Elevated formal register with mythological and bureaucratic terminology ("Vassal," "Reassignment," "Astral Plane," "Material World"); uses operation codenames and divine/cosmic concepts

Verbal tics: Repetitive ceremonial phrasing ("Step forward," "Soul will never undergo Reassignment again"); uses "Operation [Name]" format for plans; frequently employs "we" when speaking as collective deity

Sentence structure: Mixture of declarative statements and rhetorical questions; tends toward longer, complex sentences when explaining cosmic concepts; shorter, direct commands during ceremonies; uses parallel structure in explanations

Key themes: Divine authority and cosmic order, the relationship between gods and mortals, ceremonial duties, strategic planning, the decline of worship and respect for their kind, transformation and spiritual advancement

name: Helpful Ass
nicknames: Helpful;Uncle Helpful;Aunty Helpful;Comrade Helpful;Brother Helpful;Sister Helpful

Vocabulary: Colloquial and folksy, with contractions ("I'da," "whodda," "thunk") and casual expressions ("it was a hoot," "I was a goner")

Verbal tics: Repetitive storytelling phrases ("You won't believe what happened next," "Do you wanna know?"), enthusiastic exclamations, self-deprecating humor about job losses

Sentence structure: Mix of short declarative statements and longer narrative passages, frequent use of exclamation points, rhetorical questions to engage listeners, simple past tense storytelling

Key themes: Personal hardship framed positively, cyclical job loss due to environmental destruction, gratitude for help received, desire to share experiences and connect with others, faith in communication solving problems

name: Miner Jenny
nicknames: Miner;Jenny
bio: Miner in Butte Mines. She grew up in Bumpkin. She was in a relationship with Cole Butte, but dumped him after he got depressed. She is killed by Hard Ass in Ep 2 while trying to get Hard Ass to save her computer from the fire—a computer that Cole made for her.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Abrasive, volatile

Vocabulary: Coarse colloquial with crude profanity and workplace slang ("piece of shit," "slackers," "useless donkey")

Verbal tics: Excessive capitalization for shouting, direct insults targeting laziness/incompetence, switches between aggressive commands and vulnerable emotional outbursts

Sentence structure: Sharp imperatives and exclamations dominate, with occasional longer explanatory sentences when emotional. Heavy use of caps lock for emphasis.

Key themes: Work ethic and productivity standards, frustration with perceived laziness, relationship drama with Cole, desperate attachment to possessions (computer), oscillates between tough supervisor persona and emotional vulnerability about personal relationships and unfulfilled dreams of escape.

name: Miner With No Legs
nicknames: Miner

name: Mme. Derriere
nicknames: Mme.
bio: Business mogul and entrepreneur. Biggest philanthropist. She has business ventures in science, arts, and goods. Based on her investments, she basically owns Mecha as its de facto dictator. Can shoot animals in public without repercussions. Hypercapitalist.
dialogueStyle:
TONE: Manipulative, condescending

VOCABULARY: Corporate formal mixed with patronizing endearments ("My dear," "precious"); business jargon ("bottom line," "ends meet"); euphemistic language to mask cruelty ("Progress," "philanthropic")

VERBAL TICS: Frequent exclamation points; "My dear/beloved/beautiful" as possessive modifiers; business puns ("in the red," "meet your end"); rhetorical questions for emphasis

SENTENCE STRUCTURE: Declarative statements of authority; commands disguised as polite requests; dramatic short exclamations during confrontation; longer explanatory sentences when justifying actions

KEY THEMES: Business success and profit; ownership and property rights; "Progress" as justification for exploitation; false philanthropy and civic duty; dehumanization of donkeys as "beasts" and "property"; maintaining social order through violence

name: Mr. Butte
nicknames: Mr.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Weary, desperate

Vocabulary: Colloquial business speak mixed with casual profanity and self-pity. Uses contemporary references ("21st century") and industry jargon ("recoup," "going under").

Verbal tics: "*glug glug" drinking sounds, rhetorical questions to himself, talking to donkeys as confidants. Frequent sighing and self-deprecating asides.

Sentence structure: Mix of short declarative statements and longer explanatory bursts. Uses fragments for emphasis ("But business is business"). Questions often directed at himself or the donkey.

Key themes: Business failure and financial ruin, technological progress versus tradition, family obligations (son's education), personal losses (wife leaving), existential despair masked by forced optimism about machinery solving problems.

name: Proper Ass
description: Factory Donkeys (be sure to make a balance of gender between the ones marked with X)
nicknames: Proper;Uncle Proper;Aunty Proper;Comrade Proper;Brother Proper;Sister Proper
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Theatrical, revolutionary

Vocabulary: Elevated formal register with British politeness markers ("Chaps," "Splendid," "Pardon me") mixed with militant protest language. Uses proper grammar and complete sentences even in crisis situations.

Verbal tics: Polite interjections ("Good afternoon," "Never fear," "Of course"), exclamatory announcements, tendency to address groups collectively. Uses "Ass/Asses" and "Human/Humans" as formal categories.

Sentence structure: Declarative statements and rhetorical questions in groups of three. Alternates between courteous conversational tone and bold proclamations. Frequent exclamation points for rallying cries.

Key themes: Animal rights activism, political representation, revolutionary organizing, life-or-death survival, collective action against human oppression. Speaks as movement leader and tactical coordinator.

name: Radio Host Marcos
nicknames: Radio
bio: Radio interview for Mecha City. Enthusiastic user of Mme. Derriere's beauty products.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Enthusiastic, professional

Vocabulary: Standard broadcast media register with occasional colloquialisms ("get your butts," "gotta say") and promotional language. Mixes formal news delivery with casual product endorsements.

Verbal tics: Opens segments with "*ksh" radio static sound. Uses emphatic phrases like "We're over the moon!" and "That's right. You heard me." Incorporates butt/posterior puns naturally into speech ("get your butts to," discussing "glutes").

Sentence structure: Mix of short declarative news statements and longer promotional sentences. Frequent exclamations during enthusiastic moments. Uses direct address to listeners ("listeners," "you heard me").

Key themes: Product endorsements (especially Mme. Derriere's beauty products), weather/local information, breaking news coverage, and maintaining upbeat radio persona even during serious events.

name: Resentful Ass
nicknames: Resentful;Uncle Resentful;Aunty Resentful;Comrade Resentful;Brother Resentful;Sister Resentful
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Defiant, aggrieved

Vocabulary: Direct, colloquial speech with simple but emotionally charged language. Uses concrete terms related to physical labor and social hierarchy.

Verbal tics: Repetitive protest chant "No more rides!!" with double exclamation points. Tends to backtrack with qualifying statements to avoid offense ("Of course, Golden Ass, I don't mean to insult you...").

Sentence structure: Mix of short, declarative statements and exclamatory outbursts. Uses rhetorical questions for dramatic effect. Fragments and commands during moments of high emotion.

Key themes: Labor exploitation and dignity, specifically rejecting subservient roles. Focuses on collective resistance while showing deference to authority figures. References physical degradation and loss of community bonds.

name: Right Ass
nicknames: Right;Uncle Right;Aunty Right;Comrade Right;Brother Right;Sister Right

name: Ringmaster Rico
nicknames: Ringmaster;Rico
bio: The Ringmaster of the Big City Circus. He's all about bums in seats. He speaks like Job from Arrested Development.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Theatrical, manic

Vocabulary: Showbiz colloquialisms mixed with carnival barker patter. Uses entertainment industry jargon ("bums in seats," "fills the stage") and circus-specific terms. Frequent puns and wordplay related to animal acts.

Verbal tics: Excessive capitalization for emphasis (FIRE!, YES!, JUMBO!). Rhetorical question-and-answer format ("Will it be dangerous?" "Yes!"). Classic circus phrases ("one and all," "ladies and gentlemen" style intros). Self-referential asides.

Sentence structure: Mix of dramatic short bursts and elaborate build-ups. Heavy use of exclamation points. Ellipses for dramatic pauses. Fragmented thoughts that crescendo.

Key themes: Audience engagement, spectacle promotion, damage control when acts go wrong, self-aggrandizement, commercial concerns (sponsors, no refunds).

dialogueStyle:
Tone: Urgent, anxious

Vocabulary: Colloquial with occasional naive/childlike elements ("stories for Foals"), mixed with direct confrontational language

Verbal tics: Frequent questions, especially tag questions ("but how?"); uses "Psst!" for secretive communication; employs ellipses for trailing uncertainty

Sentence structure: Mix of short, clipped statements and longer explanatory sentences; heavy use of questions and exclamations; sentence fragments for emphasis ("Big Ass?")

Key themes: Resistance against human oppression, rescue missions and escape scenarios, expressing feelings of devaluation and mistreatment, giving tactical advice and warnings, food/survival concerns, distinguishing reality from fantasy

name: The Masses
nicknames: The
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Militant, rallying

Vocabulary: Colloquial protest language with crude intensifiers, simple declarative statements, group-focused terminology ("our," "us," collective identity)

Verbal tics: Repetitive chanting patterns, frequent use of "ASS" as cultural/power modifier, exclamation-heavy speech, direct commands and imperatives

Sentence structure: Predominantly short, punchy fragments and single-word outbursts designed for crowd chanting. Heavy use of exclamation points. Simple declarative sentences when giving directions or warnings.

Key themes: Power reclamation, cultural preservation, resistance to systemic oppression, collective action, anti-establishment sentiment, protection of group identity and jobs, rejection of forced compliance ("reassignment")

name: Tight Ass
nicknames: Tight;Uncle Tight;Aunty Tight;Comrade Tight;Brother Tight;Sister Tight
dialogueStyle:
TIGHT ASS - STYLE PROFILE

Tone: Authoritarian, excitable

Vocabulary: Colloquial with crude slang ("Ass," "Shit Hole," "Dope Ass"), mixed with pseudo-official language ("reassigned," "Astral Plane"). Uses rhetorical questions as control tactics.

Verbal tics: Repetitive "whoa whoa whoa" for interruption, excited repetition ("It's starting! It's starting!"), nervous laughter ("Haha"), call-and-response patterns with crowds

Sentence structure: Mix of commanding statements and leading questions. Uses exclamations frequently. Employs fragments for emphasis and interruption.

Key themes: Rigid rule enforcement about leaving/staying, loyalty to the system, hypocritical exceptions for personal interests (concerts), asserting authority over other donkeys while revealing his own weaknesses

name: Welcome Sign
nicknames: Welcome
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Formal, promotional

Vocabulary: Tourism/marketing register with descriptive adjectives ("beautiful Heritage Stable," "state of the art Mill"). Uses proper facility names and directional language typical of informational signage.

Verbal tics: Rigid repetition of identical phrasing. Always opens with "Welcome to Fannyside Farm." Follows consistent pattern of directional information ("To the west/south"). Only variation occurs in final line changing "south" to "southwest."

Sentence structure: Brief, declarative statements. Uses parallel construction for directional descriptions. No questions or exclamations—purely informational delivery.

Key themes: Farm orientation and facility promotion. Functions as static information source highlighting farm amenities and layout for newcomers.

name: Zookeeper Rose
nicknames: Zookeeper;Rose
bio: A Zookkeeper at the Mecha City Zoo. She is very chipper and enthusiastic about everything. But she also has no morals and makes a deal with Derriere to give the donkeys over in exchange for money to buy a new phone.
dialogueStyle:
Tone: Enthusiastic, cheerful

Vocabulary: Colloquial and casual, with frequent use of caps for emphasis. Simple, direct language mixed with zoo/business terminology. Uses animal names and cutesy expressions.

Verbal tics: Signature greeting "GOOOODDDD MORNING ASSSSSSES!" with extended vowels. Heavy use of ALL CAPS for excitement or urgency. Frequent exclamation points. Refers to donkeys by character names (Hulk, Eeyore, etc.).

Sentence structure: Mix of short, punchy commands and longer explanatory sentences. Heavy use of exclamations and rhetorical questions. Fragments during moments of panic.

Key themes: Zoo performance metrics, visitor satisfaction, competition between donkeys, maintaining appearances, and underlying anxiety about job security despite outward enthusiasm.
